title: Chicken Stock
cScaleSummary: C1 because the stock is made from chicken bones, drumsticks, neck, and attached tissues, which contribute some cholesterol during extraction even though the fat is removed after chilling. Defatting improves the profile, but does not make the finished stock cholesterol-free.
ldlActiveSummary: LDL-Active is false because this is a foundational stock rather than a fiber-forward dish built from legumes, whole grains, vegetables, nuts, or seeds. It can support LDL-friendly cooking, but on its own it is neutral rather than actively LDL-lowering.

items.prep: in addition to the 2 on the chicken

stepGroups.title: Directions I – Pressure Extraction
items: Add the drumsticks to the pressure cooker.

Cut the chicken into parts. Reserve the breasts & thighs for something else. Add the wings, drumsticks, and carcass to the pressure cooker. (If you remove the skin and bones from the thighs & breasts, add the skin & bones to the pressure cooker too).
Add the cold tap water to the pressure cooker and the vinegar.
Bring to 10 pounds of pressure and cook under pressure for 50 minutes.
Shut off heat and let pressure cooker cool naturally (don’t release steam).

stepGroups.title: Directions II – Aromatic Simmer
items: When cool enough to open, but still hot, pour the contents of the pressure cooker into a large stock pot.
Add the remaining ingredients, including the hot water.

stepGroups.title: Cooling and Storage
items: Place the pot of strained stock into a sink or large basin filled with ice and cold water. Stir occasionally to release heat and speed cooling.
Once the stock has cooled substantially, transfer it to the refrigerator and chill overnight.
By morning the fat will have risen and hardened on top. Lift off and discard the fat.
When chilled, the stock should set into a soft gel and jiggle when shaken. That’s the sign of good collagen extraction and a stock that will give sauces and soups real body.
Store in the refrigerator for several days or freeze in containers for longer storage.
